steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Salman-Engin's key finding (Text 1): prior research focused on ongoing relationships, but their study showed that brief interactions with strangers — warmly thanking a shuttle driver — also produce positive well-being effects. The model in Text 2 organizes connections as an individual's social network ties across three concentric circles of closeness. The question asks what Salman-Engin would say about that model.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'It underscores that most research on social interactions fails to capture a category of connection that has the capacity to contribute positively to individuals' sense of well-being.': The model only represents existing social network ties — strangers outside the network are entirely absent. Salman-Engin, who showed stranger interactions are beneficial, would note that the model (and the research it reflects) misses this category. ✓
  • Choice B — 'It reflects an overemphasis on relationship longevity...': Salman-Engin's study is about stranger interactions, not relationship duration. Longevity is not their concern. ✗
  • Choice C — 'It explains researchers' observations that individuals typically expect interactions with familiar people to be more positive...': This reverses Salman-Engin's finding — they found stranger interactions are positive, not that familiar interactions are expected to be more positive. ✗
  • Choice D — 'It emphasizes distinctions among types of close connections...': Salman-Engin's concern is with strangers entirely outside any social network, not with distinctions among close vs. casual ties within one. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
